intro:
Sarah (“princess”; formerly Sarai) was the wife and half-sister of Abraham (Genesis 20:12) and the mother of Isaac — the matriarch through whom God’s covenant promise of offspring was carried. When the LORD confirmed the covenant, he changed her name from Sarai to Sarah and declared, “I will bless her, and… she shall become nations; kings of peoples shall come from her” (Genesis 17:15-16). A woman of striking beauty, she was twice endangered by Abraham’s half-truth that she was his sister, and twice protected by God’s intervention before foreign kings (Genesis 12:10-20; Genesis 20:1-18).

For most of her life Sarah was barren, and the long delay tested her faith. Despairing of bearing a child herself, she gave her Egyptian servant Hagar to Abraham; the resulting birth of Ishmael brought rivalry and grief into the household (Genesis 16:1-6). When the LORD announced that Sarah herself would bear a son within the year, she laughed in disbelief — “After I am worn out… shall I have pleasure?” — and was gently reproved, “Is anything too hard for the LORD?” (Genesis 18:11-14).

God kept his word: at the appointed time the aged Sarah bore Isaac — whose name means “he laughs” — and her doubting laughter turned to joy: “God has made laughter for me” (Genesis 21:1-7). Fiercely protective of the child of promise, she insisted that Hagar and Ishmael be sent away, which God confirmed to Abraham (Genesis 21:9-12). She died at 127 — the only woman whose age at death the Bible records — and Abraham buried her in the cave of Machpelah at Hebron, Israel’s first foothold in the land of promise (Genesis 23:1-2).

The New Testament holds Sarah up as a pattern of faith and godliness. Hebrews lists her among the faithful: “By faith Sarah herself received power to conceive… since she considered him faithful who had promised” (Hebrews 11:11). Peter commends her trustful obedience as a model for believing women (1 Peter 3:5-6). And Paul makes Sarah, the free woman whose son was born “through promise,” an allegory of the new covenant and the heavenly Jerusalem, over against Hagar and the bondage of the law (Galatians 4:22-31) — so that all who are children of promise are reckoned her offspring.
hitchcock_meaning: lady; princess; princess of the multitude
